whyHot: The single most-recommended bass shaker of 2026 across sim-racing guides; repeatedly named the best-value first tactile unit — the balance of ~$80-120 price and 50W punch that everyone tells newcomers to buy first.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has ONE haptic (ButtKicker Gamer PRO). Tactile is the #1 immersion upgrade the community pushes after a base+pedals. The BST-1 is the canonical entry point — needs its own product + a 'how tactile works' explainer.

whyHot: 2026 'Editor's Choice' for maximum immersion — 300W RMS; reviewers call it a smarter pick than the ButtKicker Mini LFE with more power, better cooling and a lower price.
notesForCatalog: The step-up tactile unit for people who outgrow the BST-1. Pairs with a dedicated amp (see Fosi). Good 'upgrade path' story alongside BST-1.

product: Next Level Racing HF8 Haptic Gaming Pad
whyHot: The community's go-to plug-and-play tactile answer — 8 vibration motors in a seat pad, telemetry-driven via SimHub, and crucially QUIET, so apartment racers who can't run bass shakers still get feedback. Boosted Media calls it 'affordable haptic feedback.'
notesForCatalog: The 'no-mounting, no-wiring, no-amp' tactile solution — perfect for the apartment/quiet-house buyer. Different job than a bass shaker; belongs as its own catalog line, not a shaker variant.

product: Next Level Racing HF8 Pro Haptic Feedback Pad
whyHot: 2026 refresh generating fresh buzz — stronger, more responsive motors (75fps response), better materials; reviewers say the timing accuracy on gear shifts and ABS pulses is a real step up over the original.
notesForCatalog: New-2026 premium version of the HF8. Good 'good/better' pairing with the standard HF8.

whyHot: The brand that pioneered bass shakers and still the default name search — 250W, deep low-end. Community caveat is real and worth documenting: it overheats without a beefy 200W+ amp and proper cooling.
notesForCatalog: Brand-recognition magnet. Catalog already has the ButtKicker Gamer PRO; the Mini LFE serves the 'build your own multi-shaker rig' crowd. Note the thermal caveat honestly.

whyHot: The cheap entry into tactile — ~$15-25 each, so racers buy 2-4 and distribute them under the seat/pedal deck. Recurring 'start here for almost nothing' recommendation.
notesForCatalog: The absolute-cheapest tactile on-ramp. Great for a 'try tactile for $50' angle; drives amp + wiring attach-sales.

product: Fosi Audio (BT20A / TP-02 class) tactile amp
whyHot: The amp is the missing half of every bass-shaker build; Fosi budget amps are the recurring 2026 pairing recommendation to actually drive a BST-300EX or ButtKicker without overheating it.
notesForCatalog: Nobody sells this to sim racers as a bundle piece and they should — every shaker needs an amp. High attach-rate accessory the catalog completely lacks.

product: Next Level Racing ES1 Elite Seat
whyHot: Purpose-built for sim racing (not an FIA track seat) — wider bolsters, more reclined position for long stints; the recurring 'first proper seat' recommendation for cockpit builders under ~$400.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has ZERO seats. The seat is the comfort foundation of a cockpit rig; this is the mainstream sim-specific pick.

whyHot: Sparco is the aspirational real-motorsport brand; the R100 with redesigned bolsters and QRT weight-saving is the 2026 'buy the real thing' pick for GT/formula authenticity.
notesForCatalog: The premium/authenticity seat option to sit above the ES1. Real-brand halo. Note: needs a seat bracket/side-mount to fit an aluminum rig.

product: Trak Racer Alpine Racing TRX V2
whyHot: 2026 flagship 80x40mm profile rig with tool-free Formula<->GT position switching; handles DD up to 16Nm. The rig enthusiasts point to as the do-everything aluminum cockpit.
notesForCatalog: Catalog mounts skew to smaller cockpits (GT Omega Apex, Next Level GT Track). A true convertible 8020 flagship anchors the 'serious rig' tier and pairs with DD bases the catalog already carries.

whyHot: Second-gen update built on feedback from 10,000+ original TR8 owners; the community's default 'first proper cockpit after a desk mount' recommendation.
notesForCatalog: The upgrade target for anyone on the catalog's Generic Desk Clamp. Strong 'desk -> real rig' funnel piece.

product: Next Level Racing Motion Platform V3
whyHot: The best-known consumer motion entry — belt-driven, quiet, ~2-3hr install. Big buzz but community is refreshingly split: r/simracing consensus warns 2DOF is where regret concentrates and to stretch to 3DOF+.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has NO motion category. Big-ticket halo product. IMPORTANT editorial angle: document the honest 2DOF-vs-3DOF debate rather than just hyping it — that candor is a trust builder.

whyHot: The value-motion brand r/simracing actually recommends when someone insists on motion — 3DOF pitch+roll+yaw (H3) up to traction-loss HERO models, at a fraction of high-end platforms. 'Skip 2DOF, start at 3DOF' points straight here.
notesForCatalog: The 'do it right' motion pick to contrast with the NLR V3. Pairs with the honest motion-buyer's-guide narrative (30% regret stat is a great hook).

product: Wind Sim Direct Kit (Sim3D) / SimWind-class 2-fan kit
whyHot: Speed-reactive wind (dual ~179cfm fans) driven by SimHub / Sim Racing Studio is a rising 'cheap immersion' category — adds a real sense of speed for a modest spend, and integrates with software builders already run.
notesForCatalog: Whole category missing. High-immersion-per-dollar; natural cross-sell with SimHub content and tactile. Note quiet-fan option (AC Infinity) for apartment builders.

whyHot: The default 'one monitor that feels like triples' pick for 2026 — 240Hz QD-OLED; dropped to ~$799 in spring sales, which reignited recommendations as the value super-ultrawide.
notesForCatalog: Catalog lists the Odyssey OLED G9 49 already — keep, but frame around the 2026 price drop and the 'ultrawide vs triples' decision, which is the real buyer question.

whyHot: New 2026 flagship — 45" 5K2K (5120x2160), 800R curve, Dual Mode, class-leading 125 PPI. The buzzy 'next-gen ultrawide' that fixes the soft-text complaint of older 1440p ultrawides.
notesForCatalog: Net-new 2026 halo display. Higher pixel density than the G9 — the aspirational ultrawide pick. Note it needs a strong GPU (ties to the GPU entry).

product: Next Level Racing Free Standing Triple Monitor Stand
whyHot: The default triples stand — 61lb powder-coated steel, supports 32-65" screens for ~195 degrees of vision, rock-solid with no wobble. Recurring 'buy the stand that won't flex' recommendation.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has ZERO monitor stands. Triple owners need this and it's a high-margin attach to the monitor. Free-standing (vs rig-mounted) is the community-preferred approach to isolate screen vibration.

product: VIVO Freestanding Triple Monitor Desk Mount
whyHot: The budget-favorite triples mount with independent swivel/tilt/rotation per screen — the pick for mismatched or curved displays; huge review counts signal broad community trust.
notesForCatalog: Value counterpart to the NLR stand. Amazon-friendly, high-volume affiliate item for budget triples builders.

whyHot: The 2026 resolution benchmark for sim VR — the pick when you want the highest pixel count and have the GPU for it. Named the top-end target above the Crystal Light most racers land on.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has the Crystal Light (value) + Quest 3 (entry). The Crystal Super is the aspirational top of the VR ladder — completes the 3-tier VR story.

whyHot: The 2026 answer 'when weight matters more than wireless' — featherweight micro-OLED for long endurance stints without neck fatigue, a top complaint about the Quest 3.
notesForCatalog: Distinct VR value prop (comfort/weight for endurance racers). Caveat to document: needs external base stations + separate controllers.

whyHot: The connective tissue of a multi-rim setup — swap between a formula rim and a GT round wheel in seconds. Cheap (~$49) and cross-compatible across the MOZA ecosystem the catalog already sells.
notesForCatalog: QR/adapters are invisible-but-essential for the multi-wheel owner (which the catalog's 9 wheels imply). Enables '70mm PCD third-party rim' cross-sells.

whyHot: The 2026 'best premium rim' recommendation for the 70mm-PCD open-standard crowd — licensed McLaren feel, integrated dash. The aspirational rim once you own a good base.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has Ascher F28-SC + Cube Controls already; the Artura Pro is the current halo. Pair with the QR entry so buyers see how a base + QR + premium rim ecosystem fits.

product: Elgato Stream Deck MK.2 (15-key) + SimHub plugin
whyHot: The 2026 'race deck' — the SimHub Stream Deck plugin makes keys two-way (pit-limiter key lights when limiter's on, tyre key flips colour when cold). Huge community mindshare for single-screen/triples racers.
notesForCatalog: Zero button-box/Stream Deck coverage in catalog. Massive content hook (SimHub integration is a whole tutorial). Caveat: useless in VR (can't see labels) — document the button-box-vs-Stream-Deck choice.

product: Apex Sim Racing GTE button box (5" telemetry display)
whyHot: 2026 buyer's-guide pick for a physical button box with an integrated 5" SimHub display — muscle-memory switches that work in VR (unlike a Stream Deck) plus live gear/delta/tyre-temp readout.
notesForCatalog: The VR-friendly control counterpart to the Stream Deck. Physical-switch crowd + dashboard-in-one. Complements the SimHub dashboard entry.

product: SimHub-driven dash screen (e.g. USB DDU / tablet mount)
whyHot: SimHub is the software that ties nearly every dashboard build together; a dedicated dash/DDU screen showing fuel, deltas, tyre temps and shift lights is a top 'looks-pro, races-better' upgrade discussed constantly in 2026.
notesForCatalog: Whole telemetry-display category is missing. Strong content pillar (SimHub setup) + hardware (dash screens, USB rev displays). Bridges software immersion with a physical buy.

whyHot: The most-hyped pedal tech of the era — 1100N software-defined force feedback that simulates ABS, lockup and traction loss in the pedal itself. Owners say 'can't imagine going back to passive pedals.'
notesForCatalog: Catalog already has Simucube ActivePedal Pro + MOZA mBooster — keep, but this is THE aspirational pedal story; make sure the active-pedal explainer exists, since it's a whole new sub-category most buyers don't understand yet.

whyHot: The 2026 sweet spot for triple 1440p and high-res VR — holds 120Hz on a full iRacing grid where the 5070 Ti dips, and is the minimum credible pick for Pimax Crystal Light native render. The GPU the community keeps naming.
notesForCatalog: The rig is useless without a PC and the catalog ignores compute entirely. The GPU is the single biggest driver of whether triples/VR are even viable — huge content + affiliate opportunity, and a natural 'what PC do I need' pillar.

whyHot: The 2026 value baseline — locks 144Hz at high settings across triple 1080p even at full iRacing grids, at ~$550-650. The 'don't overspend, the 5090 sleeps through iRacing' community pick.
notesForCatalog: The value GPU pick to pair with the 5080 aspirational. Anchors an honest 'you don't need a 5090' guide — that candor ranks well and builds trust.

whyHot: 2026 top headset pick for sim racing — wireless freedom, all-day comfort, solid spatial audio. The default recommendation for apartment/endurance/VR-adjacent racers who need positional sound without disturbing others.
notesForCatalog: No audio/headset category in catalog. Positional audio is a genuine performance aid (hearing a car alongside). High-volume Amazon affiliate. Pair with a 'headphones vs speakers vs bass shaker' explainer.

whyHot: Recurring 2026 pick for pinpoint positional audio via THX Spatial + fast 2.4GHz wireless — the 'hear exactly where the car is' headset esports-minded racers reach for.
notesForCatalog: Second headset option (positional-accuracy angle). Rounds out the audio tier.

product: Govee RGBIC Gaming Light Bars (with sim-rig mount)
whyHot: The community-favorite 'cheap game-changer' for rig ambience — RGBIC light bars (often reactive to on-screen action) clamped to the cockpit; described as premium-feeling and a genuine mood upgrade for the space.
notesForCatalog: No lighting category exists. Cheap, photogenic, high-attach, Amazon-friendly. Sells the 'complete the room' experience beyond the hardware. Note aftermarket rig-mount brackets exist for the aluminum-profile crowd.

whyHot: The unglamorous-but-universally-loved rig tidy-up — a profile-mounted box that hides the power bricks and cable nest. Recurring 'wish I'd done this sooner' quality-of-life buy.
notesForCatalog: Zero cable-management coverage. Every multi-device rig has a cable-nest problem; low price, high satisfaction, good 'finish your build' content.

whyHot: OC Racing's #1 'cheap accessory that improved my setup more than expected' — a modern DD base + pedals + wheel + button box + shifter overwhelms motherboard USB; a powered hub kills random disconnects and centralizes cabling.
notesForCatalog: The classic 'nobody thinks of it until their wheel disconnects mid-race' item. High-satisfaction sleeper; Amazon affiliate; solves a real, documented pain.

product: Logitech K400 Plus wireless keyboard + trackpad
whyHot: OC Racing calls it 'practically the sim racing keyboard' — one lightweight lap-friendly unit for menu navigation, huge battery life, cheap. A recurring sleeper QoL pick.
notesForCatalog: Tiny price, universal recommendation. Perfect 'complete the experience' filler that no hero-item catalog bothers to list. Amazon-friendly.

product: Sim rig cup holder + phone/tablet mount
whyHot: The comfort/QoL cluster OC Racing highlights — cup holder + accessory mounts make a rig 'feel more complete' and support long-session endurance racing. Small spend, disproportionate satisfaction.
notesForCatalog: The finishing-touches category. Cheap, high-margin, Amazon-friendly cluster (cup holder, phone mount, keyboard tray). Rounds out 'complete the experience' breadth.

category: Seat slider / keyboard tray / mouse tray
product: Aluminum-profile keyboard/mouse tray + seat slider add-ons
whyHot: Profile-mount add-ons (keyboard/mouse deck, seat slider) are the recurring 'make the 8020 rig actually livable' upgrades builders add after the frame — enable non-race PC use and quick position tweaks.
notesForCatalog: Attach-sale to the aluminum-rig entries. The 'my rig is also my desk' crowd is large; these are the enabling add-ons.

product: Next Level Racing Flight Simulator (Microsoft Flight Simulator Edition)
whyHot: Official Microsoft Flight Simulator partnership rig launched 2025-26 with the industry's first flight-tuned integrated lumbar seat; the reference 'buy the whole cockpit' answer forums point new commercial-flyers to.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has zero seating/rig entries. This is the aspirational anchor for anyone graduating from a desk clamp to a dedicated cockpit — huge basket-builder and high ticket.

product: Next Level Racing Flight Simulator Lite (NLR-S022)
whyHot: The folding, apartment-friendly rig repeatedly recommended for people with limited space who still want a rigid cockpit-style seat instead of a desk clamp.
notesForCatalog: The affordable/foldable entry into the seating category — the realistic first-rig purchase for most simmers who can't dedicate a room.

product: SimFab Modular Flight Simulator Cockpit (DCS / Rotorcraft Edition)
whyHot: Community-favorite modular rig with pre-drilled brackets that accept Thrustmaster Warthog, WinWing, VIRPIL, VKB and Komodo hardware, and upgrades between fixed-wing, heli and space configs — the flexible sleeper pick vs the big brands.
notesForCatalog: For the builder who wants one frame that flexes across fixed-wing/heli/space and mounts any brand of hardware. Pairs with the collective bracket below.

whyHot: The go-to add-on bracket for mounting a WinWing / VIRPIL / Komodo collective to a rig — solves the perennial 'how do I mount my collective' question in the heli community.
notesForCatalog: Accessory that unlocks the (expensive) collective purchase — catalog has a collective but no way to mount it. Attach-rate item.

product: MonsterTech MTSIM Joystick / HOTAS Table Mount
whyHot: The universally cited premium desk-clamp: BESSEY quick-release clamp rated to 2500 N, anodized aluminum, infinitely height-adjustable — the answer to 'my base slides during aggressive maneuvers.'
notesForCatalog: Catalog has no mounting solutions at all. This is the single most-recommended way to get a stick/throttle off a wobbly desk without buying a full rig — a very common upgrade.

product: Hikig / J-PEIN HOTAS Desk Clamp Pair
whyHot: The budget desk-clamp repeatedly recommended as the 'good enough for a fraction of MonsterTech' entry to getting hardware off the desk surface.
notesForCatalog: Value-tier mounting for cost-conscious builders; broadens the mount category with an entry price point below MonsterTech.

whyHot: Called the first headset where cockpit instruments 'finally come into focus' — 3840x3840 per eye, 50 PPD; the 2026 clarity benchmark that flight simmers rave about for reading gauges without leaning in.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has zero VR. VR is arguably THE immersion upgrade for flight sim. This is the halo product for enthusiasts with an RTX 4090/5090.

whyHot: At 107g the lightest PCVR headset made; Micro-OLED true blacks win praise for night landings and long-haul legibility. The comfort-first pick for multi-hour flights that the community keeps recommending.
notesForCatalog: The 'comfort king' VR pick — long flights make weight the #1 complaint, and this solves it. Distinct buyer from the Crystal Super.

whyHot: The default budget/accessible entry into VR flight — every 'should I get VR' thread lands here first; great for VFR even if small cockpit text is a stretch. Huge install base.
notesForCatalog: The most-bought VR headset overall and the on-ramp for most simmers. Highest-volume VR entry — anchors the category's affordable end.

product: BOBOVR M3 Pro Head Strap with Battery (Quest 3)
whyHot: The near-universal first Quest 3 upgrade — reviewers and simmers swap the OEM strap for the BOBOVR to fix weight balance and add hot-swap battery for long flights.
notesForCatalog: Perfect high-margin attach item to any Quest 3 sale; directly solves the #1 Quest 3 complaint (comfort/battery). Almost everyone buys one.

whyHot: The plug-and-play tactile-feedback kit (transducer + amp) that flight simmers pair with SimShaker for Aviators to feel gear thumps, stall buffet and touchdown — repeatedly called a high-ROI immersion upgrade.
notesForCatalog: Tactile immersion is completely absent from the catalog. Bundled kit is the easiest sell — no wiring knowledge required. Cross-sells to sim racing too.

product: Dayton Audio BST-300EX 300W Tactile Bass Shaker
whyHot: The DIY community's go-to transducer for 4-corner seat/rig builds — 300W RMS, the unit experienced builders pair with a cheap Nobsound/mono amp for far more punch per dollar than a branded kit.
notesForCatalog: The value/DIY tactile pick — pairs with the amp below. Appeals to the tinkerer segment that scoffs at ButtKicker pricing.

product: Nobsound NS-01G PRO Mini Amplifier (for bass shakers)
whyHot: The cheap Class-D amp everyone recommends to drive a bare Dayton/Douk shaker on a sim rig — the missing piece that makes the DIY tactile build actually work.
notesForCatalog: Completes the DIY tactile trio (shaker + amp + software). Small ticket but essential attach — nobody buys a bare shaker without an amp.

whyHot: The telemetry-driven software that turns a bass shaker into aircraft-specific feedback (gear, flaps, touchdown, stall) for MSFS, X-Plane, DCS, IL-2 — the piece that makes tactile hardware meaningful for flight (not just racing).
notesForCatalog: Software attach that justifies every tactile hardware sale. Even at low affiliate value it strengthens the 'complete tactile setup' story on-page.

whyHot: The long-standing head-tracking standard for non-VR simmers; still the default recommendation for looking around the cockpit and checking six in DCS without a headset.
notesForCatalog: Already in catalog (flight-extra). Listed here to confirm head-tracking is the non-VR immersion path and to anchor the category next to Tobii.

whyHot: WinWing single-handedly crashed the price of high-fidelity Airbus glareshield hardware; the FCU+EFIS combo is the centerpiece of nearly every affordable A320 home-cockpit build in 2026.
notesForCatalog: Already in catalog. Confirms WinWing panels are the hot brand; keep prominent and cross-link to the MIP MCDU/CDU units.

product: WinWing (WINCTRL) OVHD32 SOP Airbus Overhead Panel
whyHot: Announced at FSWeekend 2026 — the overhead panel simmers have been begging for; completes the Airbus home-cockpit stack (FCU/EFIS/MCDU already existed) and is generating major buzz.
notesForCatalog: Brand-new 2026 release, ships June 2026. Overhead panels are a category the catalog lacks entirely — the missing piece of the full-Airbus-cockpit story.

whyHot: Billed as the world's first DIRECT-DRIVE force-feedback joystick base for flight sim; the single biggest hardware story of FSWeekend 2026 and the category everyone says is the next revolution after the MOZA/VPForce wave.
notesForCatalog: Ships late April 2026. Force feedback is the trending frontier; catalog has a MOZA FFB yoke but no FFB stick. This is the buzz product to lead with.

whyHot: The affordable entry into force feedback at FSWeekend 2026 — brings 'pro-grade FFB to the consumer market at competitive prices'; the value door into a category that used to start near $1k.
notesForCatalog: Value FFB tier — the realistic first FFB purchase. Distinct price/buyer from the DB16 direct-drive flagship.

whyHot: MOZA's 12Nm FFB flagship; the other half of the 2026 force-feedback arms race the community obsesses over, cross-compatible with MOZA's racing ecosystem.
notesForCatalog: Gives the FFB category a second brand and lets MOZA-ecosystem owners (who may already have a MOZA racing base) cross-shop. Trending head-to-head with CyberTaurus.

whyHot: The 2026 best-value combat stick everyone recommends: metal build, 6 axes, 26 buttons, handles photogrammetry approaches cleanly at the budget tier — the new default 'first good stick.'
notesForCatalog: The most-recommended budget stick of 2026 and pairs with the Ursa Minor Fighter Throttle. Catalog's sticks are Thrustmaster/Turtle Beach — WinWing is the trending value gap.

whyHot: Perennial community 'best mid-range stick' — ball-bearing gimbal with smooth precise center that both fighter jocks and helicopter pilots single out.
notesForCatalog: The evergreen enthusiast stick recommendation, and the go-to for heli cyclic feel. Catalog has no VKB stick — clear brand gap.

whyHot: The precision benchmark rudder pedals the whole community defers to — composite + aluminum with 17 bearings and magnetic sensors; the 'endgame pedals' answer in every pedal thread.
notesForCatalog: Catalog pedals are Honeycomb/Logitech/Turtle Beach/VKB — no MFG. The aspirational pedal upgrade the community treats as the gold standard.

product: WinWing (WINCTRL) CyberTaurus EB195 Force Feedback Pedals
whyHot: New at FSWeekend 2026 — force-feedback rudder pedals (195N) extending the FFB revolution to the feet; novel category with real buzz for realistic anti-torque/crosswind feel.
notesForCatalog: Ships late April 2026. A genuinely new sub-category (FFB pedals) — differentiates the catalog's pedal lineup with cutting-edge tech.

whyHot: The reference helicopter control set — Asobo/Microsoft used it in MSFS 2024 heli reveals; folding, all-metal cyclic + collective + anti-torque pedals. The definitive heli answer as MSFS 2024 pushed helicopters mainstream.
notesForCatalog: Helicopters are surging in MSFS 2024. Catalog has one Komodo collective but no complete heli set or cyclic. This is the halo heli product.

product: VIRPIL VPC Rotor TCS Plus Collective (with Hawk-60 Grip)
whyHot: The enthusiast/DCS heli community's premium modular collective — adjustable friction and swappable grips; the 'did I make the right choice — yes' pick in MSFS heli-building threads.
notesForCatalog: Second collective option for the serious heli builder; VIRPIL is a marquee brand entirely absent from the catalog.

whyHot: The affordable, only-30mm-thick, plug-and-play CDU that undercuts the big panel brands and works with PMDG 737/777 in MSFS, X-Plane and P3D — a genuine value/sleeper the community trades notes on.
notesForCatalog: A budget alternative to WinWing MCDU for Boeing builders — widens the panel category down-market and is Amazon-affiliate friendly (in-stock, US listings).

whyHot: 1:1 Boeing 737 Mode Control Panel, standalone plug-and-play via Bridge software — the dedicated MCP the 737 home-cockpit crowd points to for real autopilot muscle memory.
notesForCatalog: Boeing-side counterpart to the WinWing Airbus FCU — catalog is Airbus-heavy on glareshield; this fills the Boeing autopilot-panel gap.

whyHot: Turns any tablet/touchscreen into interactive instrument panels and switch panels for MSFS 2020/2024, X-Plane and P3D — the software backbone of DIY glass-cockpit and touchscreen builds the community relies on.
notesForCatalog: Bridges cheap hardware (a spare tablet/monitor) into a working panel — a foundational 'software as gear' entry that supports the touchscreen-panel story.

product: Aviation Kneeboard / Gooseneck EFB Tablet Mount (for iPad Navigraph/SimBrief)
whyHot: MSFS 2024's built-in EFB now streams to a real iPad over Wi-Fi (Navigraph + SimBrief), driving demand for a desk/rig tablet mount to run charts alongside the sim — an active 'how do I mount my tablet' forum topic.
notesForCatalog: Newly relevant because MSFS 2024's EFB now works on a real iPad. Cheap, high-attach accessory; catalog has no EFB/tablet-mount category at all.

whyHot: The community consensus for non-VR immersion: triple 1440p gives ~180° horizontal FOV for VFR scanning while staying within MSFS 2024 VRAM/perf budgets — recommended over ultrawide because ultrawide distorts side references.
notesForCatalog: Displays are entirely missing from the catalog. Triple-monitor is the mainstream non-VR immersion path; frame it as a curated 'best flight-sim monitor' pick rather than one SKU.

product: 49" Curved Super-Ultrawide (32:9) Monitor for Flight Sim
whyHot: The single-screen immersion alternative for those who don't want triple-bezel hassle; 32:9 wraps the cockpit view and is a frequent 'one big screen vs triples' debate winner for IFR/airliner flyers.
notesForCatalog: Alternative display path to triples — one panel, no bezels, easier setup. Broadens the (missing) monitor category with a second buyer profile.

experienceRole: compute / GPU for demanding sim engine
whyHot: The balanced high-end GPU pick for MSFS 2024 — holds 4K Ultra with frame-gen and is the right card for high-end VR at sensible settings; the most-recommended 'don't need a 5090' answer in 2026 build threads.
notesForCatalog: MSFS 2024 is the most GPU-hungry sim; the PC/GPU angle is missing entirely. The 5080 is the enthusiast sweet spot — great buyer-guide anchor.

experienceRole: compute / flagship GPU for VR + triples
whyHot: The only card that comfortably clears high-resolution VR (Pimax Crystal Super) and photogrammetry-dense MSFS 2024 at once; the endgame GPU every high-fidelity VR flyer covets despite scarce stock.
notesForCatalog: The halo GPU that makes the halo VR headsets usable. Pairs narratively with the Pimax Crystal Super entry — 'what you need to actually run it.'

whyHot: Home cockpits chew through USB ports (yoke, throttle, pedals, panels, MCDU, head-tracker, shaker); a powered hub is the unglamorous must-have every multi-panel builder ends up buying to stop device dropouts.
notesForCatalog: Catalog already has a Desktop Pilot 10-port hub. Confirmed as a foundational accessory — keep it and cross-sell alongside every panel.

product: Cable Sleeve / Velcro Tie + Under-Desk Tray Kit (flight-sim cockpit wiring)
whyHot: Multi-panel cockpits become a cable nest; wiring-tidy kits are a recurring 'finally tamed my setup' post — low-cost, high-satisfaction, and a natural bundle add to any hub/panel purchase.
notesForCatalog: Cable management is entirely absent. Cheap, universal, high-attach — ideal 'complete your setup' upsell. Frame as a curated kit rather than one SKU.

product: Gaming Headset with Clear Mic for VATSIM/ATC (e.g. HyperX / SteelSeries class)
whyHot: Online ATC networks (VATSIM/PilotEdge) and MSFS 2024 live ATC made a clear-mic headset an immersion staple; the community steers newcomers to a comfortable closed-back set with a good boom mic for radio calls.
notesForCatalog: Audio/comms is a missing category. As live/online ATC grows, a clear-mic headset is a genuine need and a high-volume affiliate item. Frame as curated pick.

whyHot: The default answer whenever anyone posts a HOSAS build asking 'how do I stop my sticks sliding around' — aluminium clamp mounts are the near-universal recommendation for turning a plain desk into a fixed dual-stick station; the pair for stick+throttle runs ~$200-250.
notesForCatalog: Catalog already lists the Monstertech HOTAS mount but treats mounting as a single SKU. Desk mounting is its own experience category — every HOSAS builder needs it, and the desk-mount vs chair-mount vs rail-mount decision is a natural buyer guide. Add as the anchor of a 'mounting' cluster.

product: Foxx Mount Joystick / Throttle Desk Mount
whyHot: The community's value alternative to Monstertech — USA-made, ~$175/pair (or $87.50 each) vs Monstertech's ~$200-250, repeatedly cited as 'just as good for less' with the tradeoff of a 5-6 week lead time.
notesForCatalog: The budget/value counterpart in any mount comparison. Great for a 'Monstertech vs Foxx' head-to-head. Likely direct-only (no Amazon), so affiliate potential is lower — but strong editorial/authority value.

whyHot: The answer for HOSAS players with no desk, standing desks, or recliner setups — clamps the sticks to the chair arms so they travel with you and sit at a natural, wrist-neutral angle. The MFC Flight Chair Configurator lets you build the exact chair rig.
notesForCatalog: Completely missing from the catalog and it's the single biggest ergonomics unlock for HOSAS — sticks at your sides on the chair beats reaching forward to a desk. Distinct enough from desk mounts to be its own product + its own 'do I mount to desk or chair' guide.

whyHot: Virpil's own space-cockpit guide calls extensions the key HOSAS ergonomics move — pairing a 50/75mm extension on the right MongoosT-50CM3 base (and a Z-Extension on the left) is the recommended dual-stick config for comfortable, precise wrist-neutral throw.
notesForCatalog: A near-invisible-to-newbies accessory that the community swears by. Cheap add-on, high attach-rate to any Virpil/VKB base already in the catalog. Perfect 'you already bought the base, here's the $50 that makes it feel right' upsell.

whyHot: Specifically recommended for converting the left stick to act as a throttle in a HOSAS rig — the offset Z shape angles the left grip for pull/push throttle use. Virpil flags it as the popular pairing with the CM3 base.
